mcq.q: How are the three Zero Trust principles best summarized?
choices: Never trust / always verify, assume breach, least privilege
Encrypt everything, log everything, patch quickly
Perimeter defense, VPN tunnelling, firewall rules
Authenticate once, then trust everything inside the network
mcq.explanation: Zero Trust is defined by never-trust/always-verify, assume-breach, and least privilege — applied to agents that act autonomously at machine speed.

mcq.q: Why do the authors say AI-accelerated offense has "raised the Foundation floor"?
choices: Friction-only controls like rotating long-lived API keys and SMS MFA no longer qualify as adequate
Foundation now mandates hardware attestation on every device
Small deployments are exempted from all security requirements
The Advanced tier was merged into Foundation
mcq.explanation: Short-lived tokens, cryptographically-rooted identity and automated triage are now entry requirements; friction-only controls no longer count.

mcq.q: What does the framework predict about its three maturity tiers over time?
choices: Advanced becomes Enterprise standard and Enterprise becomes Foundation
The tiers collapse into a single compliance checklist
Each tier replaces the controls of the tier below it
Foundation is deprecated for regulated industries
mcq.explanation: Tiers are a roadmap, not a finish line: "expect Advanced to become Enterprise standard… and Enterprise to become Foundation." Each tier strengthens, never replaces.

cards.back: Never trust / always verify; assume breach (limit blast radius); least privilege.

cards.front: Which OWASP principle extends least privilege to agents?
cards.back: Least agency — constrains what each tool can do, how often, and where.

mcq.q: Complete Karpathy's analogy: classical computers automate what you can ___; LLMs automate what you can ___.
choices: specify; verify
verify; specify
imagine; compute
label; predict
mcq.explanation: "Traditional computers automate what you can specify in code; this round of LLMs automates what you can verify."

mcq.q: Per the thesis, a capability reliably shows up when it is…
choices: Verifiable AND the labs cared enough to build environments / include the data
Verifiable, regardless of what training data exists
In the pre-training set, even if it is unverifiable
Requested by enough enterprise customers
mcq.explanation: "Verifiable + labs care" — both conditions. The chess spike happened because someone added the data; verifiability alone isn't sufficient.

mcq.q: What is the optimistic horizon — "everything is eventually verifiable"?
choices: Even soft domains like writing yield to a council of LLM judges; it's how-hard, not whether
Only math and code will ever be verifiable
Verification gets automated away entirely by AGI
Labs will stop needing RL environments
mcq.explanation: Almost anything can be made verifiable to some degree; LLM-judge ensembles extend the reward signal into fuzzy domains.

cards.front: Why does capability peak sharply in math and code?
cards.back: RL training rewards verified outcomes; the compiler/test is the verifier, and the verifier is the reward signal.

cards.front: The founder lever in the verifiability thesis
cards.back: Build a proprietary verification / RL environment for a verifiable domain the labs haven't prioritized — a cornered resource.

mcq.q: Why do LLMs degrade as context grows?
choices: Attention relationships scale quadratically (O(n²)) with token count
The GPU runs out of RAM at a fixed token count
The tokenizer exhausts its vocabulary
They degrade linearly, from slower sampling
mcq.explanation: Quadratic attention scaling creates a ~100K-token "smart zone"; beyond it the model gets dumber regardless of the advertised window.

mcq.q: What did shipping 1M-token context windows actually achieve, per Pocock?
choices: It "shipped a lot more dumb zone" — the smart zone stays ~100K
It moved the smart zone out to ~1M tokens
It removed the need to clear context
It made compaction strictly unnecessary
mcq.explanation: Long context helps retrieval, not reasoning; the smart zone is ~100K independent of the advertised window size.

choices: Compaction accumulates lossy "sediment"; clearing returns to a known-clean smart-zone baseline
Clearing preserves more conversational continuity
Compaction costs far more tokens
Clearing keeps the full history available
mcq.explanation: If a task can be resumed from a written record, clear-and-restart beats compaction's lossy summaries.

cards.back: First ~100K tokens = smart zone (model performs well); beyond it the model gets dumber regardless of window size.

cards.front: Why do sub-agents preserve the parent's context?
cards.back: A sub-agent runs in its own context window; only its summary returns, so the parent stays in its smart zone.

choices: A process that repeatedly executes a prompt until a queue empties or a stop condition is met
A single long-running context window
An automatic retry of a failed tool call
A multi-agent debate that runs until consensus
mcq.explanation: A loop re-runs a prompt to drain a backlog/queue — a primitive on par with the single-shot session.

mcq.q: What are the two loop families?
choices: Cron-scheduled loops (/loop, routines) and backlog-draining loops (Ralph Wiggum)
Foreground loops and background loops
Single-agent loops and multi-agent loops
Synchronous loops and asynchronous loops
mcq.explanation: Cron-scheduled loops re-enter on a schedule; Ralph-Wiggum loops re-run a shell script that picks the next backlog task.

mcq.q: Why does each loop iteration stay effective?
choices: Each iteration starts fresh, keeping the session in the context-window smart zone
It caches the full conversation history across runs
It uses a larger context window on every pass
It disables verification to run faster
mcq.explanation: Loops fragment hours-long work into many fresh smart-zone sessions instead of one bloated context.

cards.front: Ralph Wiggum loop in one line
cards.back: A shell script re-runs the agent on a fixed prompt that picks the next AFK backlog task, until a sentinel or the harness stops it.

cards.front: What makes a task eligible in an AFK loop?

mcq.q: How does least agency go beyond least privilege?
choices: It restricts what each tool can DO, how often, and where — not just what it can access
It is just a newer name for least privilege
It applies only to human users, not agents
It grants broader access to enable autonomy
mcq.explanation: Least privilege is access scope; least agency adds the action and frequency dimensions that matter once an identity can chain operations.

mcq.q: Which is an example of least agency?
choices: An email-summarizer tool granted no send or delete rights
Sharing one service account across all of an agent's functions
Rotating a long-lived API key every month
Granting full CRUD "just to be safe"
mcq.explanation: Capability restriction (read-only, no-send) shrinks the harmful actions reachable even with valid credentials.

mcq.q: How do least agency and blast radius relate?
choices: Least agency is the input control; blast radius is the outcome metric it shrinks
They are unrelated concepts
Blast radius is the input control; least agency is the outcome
Both are authentication mechanisms
mcq.explanation: Agency-in, blast-radius-out: constraining actions × frequency × scope is how you shrink the blast radius assume-breach expects to be tested.

cards.front: Least agency — who coined it, and what does it add?
cards.back: OWASP. It extends least privilege to agent tools: what each tool can do, how often, and where.
Splitting into multiple agents but giving them all the same credentials = failing to compartmentalize the risk.

mcq.q: What does a vertical slice cut through?
choices: A thin path through every layer (schema → service → API → UI)
All of one layer before moving to the next
Only the UI, for a fast demo
The backend, deferring all UI to the end
mcq.explanation: Vertical slices give end-to-end feedback after the first slice; horizontal slices defer feedback until the last phase.

mcq.q: Why do agents default to horizontal slicing, and why is it worse for them?
choices: It's legible layer-by-layer but yields no integrated feedback until the end, and agents lack an end-to-end mental model to catch mismatches
It is faster to execute and always correct
Agents are unable to write database migrations
Horizontal slices merge to main more frequently
mcq.explanation: Legible but feedback-deferring; agents have no in-flight mental model, so mismatches surface only at the end.

mcq.q: What does a "good" first vertical slice look like?
choices: It touches schema, a new service, a route, and a minimal UI (e.g. "award points for lesson completion, visible on the dashboard")
Just the gamification service on its own
Only the schema migrations for every feature
A full design doc with no code
mcq.explanation: Pocock's rule: the first slice should show schema changes, a new service, and a minimal representation on the front end.

cards.back: Glowing rounds let the gunner see the line and correct aim — each slice gives end-to-end signal to correct against.
Blocking-edge Kanban lets multiple agents grab non-blocked tickets in parallel; a numbered phase list forces sequential work.
